_meta.note: Built to the Armagh-locked standard. Tipperary beat Waterford in the Munster quarter-final, lost the semi-final heavily to Cork, then played a three-game Tailteann Cup campaign (beat Sligo, lost to Antrim, lost to Wicklow). Five championship games.
_meta.verification: All scorelines and venues verified against the official RTE and Munster GAA results tables and primary reports (RTE, Irish Examiner, gaa.ie). Scorer detail is partial: published per-player breakdowns were not captured for every game and honest nulls are used where the full list was not in hand.

manager.name: Niall Fitzgerald
manager.note: Made four late changes to his published team for the Wicklow Tailteann game, handing championship debuts and first starts of the year to several players (Paddy Creedon, Jimmy Feehan, Steven O'Brien).

source.snippet: Niall Fitzgerald made four late changes to the published Tipp team.

league.note: Tipperary competed in Division 4 of the National League. Exact final position not captured here.

source.snippet: those that did not reach the final of their provincial championship and were not among the seven highest-ranked teams at the end of the 2026 National Football League.
An eight-point second-half streak powered Wicklow into their third consecutive Tailteann Cup quarter-final, beating Tipperary by that very margin at Echelon Park, Aughrim.

results.note: A five-point Munster quarter-final win over Waterford (the lowest-scoring game of the 2026 Munster series, 25 points aggregate), prompted by Seán O'Connor, to set up a semi-final against Cork. Full scorer list not captured; to be filled on a verification pass.

scorers.note: 1 two-pointer; first-half goal from a sideline

source.snippet: Semi-Final 25 Apr Tipperary 1-12 4-18 Cork FBD Semple Stadium

results.note: A 15-point home semi-final defeat to a Cork side that hit four goals. Full scorer list not captured; to be filled on a verification pass. Cork's file carries the opposite perspective.

source.snippet: First Round 10 May Sligo 1-12 0-17 Tipperary Markievicz Park

results.note: A two-point Tailteann Round 1 win away to Sligo, with a couple of early two-pointers setting the tone, to reach Round 2A. Full scorer list not captured; this game is also recorded in Sligo's file. To be filled on a verification pass.

scorers.note: goal to spark the late rally

source.snippet: Round 2A 24 May Antrim 4-12 2-12 Tipperary Corrigan Park

results.note: A six-point Round 2A loss away to Antrim (Antrim's four goals decisive), dropping Tipperary into the Round 3 knockout. Full scorer list not captured; this game is also recorded in Antrim's file. To be filled on a verification pass.

scorers.note: 0-5 from play on first championship start; 1 two-pointer

source.snippet: Tipp had just two scorers from play, and three overall, as Malachy Stone was stuck tight to Seán O'Connor throughout. Paddy Creedon was their standout performer with 0-5 from play on his first championship start.

results.note: Elimination game. Wicklow weathered a first-half gale (trailing 0-7 to 1-3 to a Kevin Quinn goal) before an eight-point second-half streak put Tipperary away. Tipp had just three scorers in total and two from play, with Malachy Stone marking Seán O'Connor out of it; Paddy Creedon was the standout with 0-5 on his first championship start. Partial scorer list (Creedon confirmed; full breakdown not published). Ended Tipperary's season.

collection_meta.collected_by: Claude (research session, not yet hand-verified by Rory)
fields_not_found: Full per-player scorer lists for four of the five games (only the Wicklow R3 standout, Paddy Creedon, is itemised; scorelines for all five games are verified)
collection_meta.notes: Tipperary's record is complete at the scoreline level for all five games but light on scorer detail, which reflects the lower-profile coverage of their Tailteann run. The arc: a workmanlike Munster QF win over Waterford, a heavy semi-final loss to Cork, then a Tailteann campaign that flickered (beat Sligo) before fading (lost to Antrim, then to Wicklow). Seán O'Connor is the recurring attacking reference point; Paddy Creedon emerged late. A verification pass should fill the four missing scorer lists from primary reports. Note: do not confuse with the strong 2026 Tipperary HURLING season (different code, different story).
